@NikolajBjorner: I am extremely grateful for your quick and extensive responses. 

I understand now that in Z3 the push-pop machinery only saves on pre-processing, 
but the internal state is not preserved.  Our positive experience with the push-pop machinery stemmed from working with Yices 1.

In the distant past I have worked in the planning domain, where I used the CPLEX library 
for optimizing linear goal functions. A major breakthrough occurred, when we reused a previous solution (which could be externalized and read back in) as a starting point for further computations. 
The incremental search was much faster than searching from scratch. We beneficially used this facility to add further constraints. It also worked well when we replaced constraints with new ones 
that possibly were contradictory to the original CSP.

That brings me to a question: can we get Z3 to dump the current internal state, and can it
read such a state back in? Can Z3 perform an incremental search in the way described above?

Let me elaborate on your first comment where you suggest to use soft constraints by using the Z3's ability to maximize an integer valued goal function, a capability that I had been unaware of.  Suppose we equate propositional variables pi with a disjunction of the domain constraints cij, and also equate propositional variables pi with constraints on bit (0-1) variables bi.

Using the following code we could compute a single solution (model) Mk that maximizes the number of domain constraints that are satisfied.

```
(assert y)

(declare-const pi Bool) ; for all i
(declare-const bi Int)  ; for all i
(assert (or (= bi 0) (= bi 1))) ; for all i, restricts bi to be a bit variable
(declare-const z Int)

(assert (= pi (= bi 1))) ; for all i
(assert (= z (+ b1 b2 ...)))
(maximize z)

(push)
(assert (=> pi (or ci1 ci2 ...))) ; for all i
(check-sat)
(get-model)
(pop)
```

We will obtain a sat solution (model) Mk, which is guaranteed to exist, because the background CSP y is known to be sat. Moreover, we can say that z >= 1, since there is at least one domain constraint cij that can be satisfied in addition to the obligatory background CSP y.

We would proceed as follows:
 
```
save the solution Mk away
inspect Mk, in order to find the domain constraints cij that are satisfied in Mk
remove these domain constraints cij from consideration
start another round (beginning with push and ending with pop), until no domain constraints cij are left
```

This algorithm is an elegant way for truly maximizing the number of domain constraints 
that are satisfied in each solution Mk. Nevertheless, it remains a heuristic when it comes to minimizing the total number K of solutions that are covering all domain constraints cij. I see no way how to express such a minimization in Z3's framework. Do you?

But, as I said before, it is not extremely important for us to truly minimize K. It is much more important to *quickly* arrive at a set of solutions Mk that (A) cover all cij and (B) whose number K is approximately minimal.

Our experience shows that for smaller CSPs y the number K of solutions equals the cardinality 
|Ci| of the largest set Ci comprising the domain constraints cij. So the algorithm above might be improved by introducing weights wi and multiplying each bit-variable bi with wi. High weigths wi would be associated with large constraint sets Ci

`(assert (= z (+ (* w1 b1) (* w2 b2) ...)))`

However, this approach would lead us into the domain of non-linearity with all the pitfalls associated with non-decidability. In any case, would Z3 allow us to enter such a non-linear optimization problem, and could its optimizer in principle work with a non-linear goal?

Again, it would not be a major hindrance for us, if only the linear optimization sketched above would work. And if the maximization of of the number of domain constraints fulfilled per solution takes too much time, we can leave it, and resort to one of the heuristics described before.

Once more I have used the push-pop mechanism, since I am familiar with it. If we could somehow 
preserve the internal state of Z3 and reuse it for the next round all the better. 

I still have to think about whether and how check-with-assumptions could be used for our purposes.

@matham thanks for the explanation, code, and for being open to discussions. I think I understand now better what you are doing. I agree that the fact that currently ``DataChunkIterators`` are processed sequentially and until completion is a limitation for what you are trying to do. The problem you are trying to solve is an important one but also a tricky one, so please allow me to dig a bit deeper with you into this. Let me ask a couple more questions to see if we can iterate to a solution:

* Why does ``write_plumb_data`` need to generate the list of “unfinished” iterators (i.e., ``datas``) each time you call the function? In the context of data streaming where you would call ``plumb_data`` many times it seems like constructing ``datas`` each time may lead to quite a bit of overhead. I’m wondering, wouldn’t it make sense to break ``write_plumb_data`` into two functions, one that constructs the list of data streams and one that writes the data streams. In this way you could reuse the list of data streams rather than having to reconstruct it every time.
* From a user perspective, I think one concern is that in the current example a user needs to change how they call the backend to do the write, i.e., instead of just ``write`` one needs to add a loop to call ``write_plumb_data`` until all data streams are exhausted. I think it would make sense to integrate this process more tightly with the API by:
   1.  adding a function that does the iteration over ``plumb_data``  
   2. extending HDF5IO.write to (optionally) process the data streams, 
   3. adding any parameters needed to HDF5IO to configure how the data streams should be handled (e.g., it could be configurable whether HDF5IO.write processes all streams to completion or whether the user will need to do this)
* How do you know right now when you need to stop calling ``plumb_data``, i.e., how do you know when all streams are exhausted, i.e., is there a function that does this?
* When do you think I/O should be flushed? What I mean is, HDF5 buffers I/O operations and if I understand the code correctly then I think the HDF5 file may end up in an invalid state after calling ``plumb_data`` and is only guaranteed to be valid once you close the file. Maybe adding an option to ``plumb_data`` to allow the user to flush I/O might be the way to go.

One thing that concerns me a bit about the current design is that it (for lack of a better word) “encourages” having multiple streams writing to the same HDF5 file. This seems potentially problematic for a couple of reasons: 
*  **write performance** the I/O performance of each stream becomes directly dependent on what the other streams are doing
*  **data corruption** if something goes wrong on one data stream it is possible that the whole file becomes corrupted and you lose the data from all streams
* **read performance** Lets say we have two streams ``A`` and ``B`` each consisting of a bunch of chunks ``ai`` and ``bi``. If both streams write to the same file then on disk you may end up with a highly fragmented file where chunks for ``A`` and ``B`` are intermixed, e.g,. ``a1, b1, a2, a3, b2, a4, b3`` . This kind of fragmentation can be problematic for the performance of data read. 

To address these problems, I think in particular in the context of data streaming it seems desirable to have the option to have each data stream go to a separate HDF5 file. To do this, we could simply have an option on the ``AbstractDataChunkIterator`` that would tell the HDF5IO backend to create a new HDF5 file to store the corresponding dataset (and attributes) and then that dataset would be linked to via an external link from the main file. This would a) avoid having to update the main file while you are streaming data, b) would make the streams independent of each other (as each could go to its on file and, hence, also allow you to process streams independently, c) help alleviate at least in part the issue of data corruption and performance. 

Once we have finalized how we want to deal with the data streaming I think we’ll also need to create some examples for this and ideally a tutorial with best practices, because this gives folks a lot of power but it can also lead to a lot of problem if one is not careful.

The relevant documentation for this is [`__repr__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html?highlight=__repr__#object.__repr__) and [`__str__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html?highlight=__repr__#object.__str__). The gist is that the `repr()` of an object should return a string which, if passed to `eval()` gives back an object just like the original. In this sense our current pretty printing is almost there (and is how `export`/`load` work). Calling `str()` on an object should return the "the 'informal' or nicely printable string representation of an object" which I guess could be identical to `repr()`, at least for proxies.

The two obvious caveats to this:
* new lines in the `repr()` output might mess things up so extra care there is needed
* objects like `jobs` should have a different `__str__` to their `_repr__` since their primary purpose is printing. Otherwise their `__repr__` can be a `<... object at ..>`-type string.

I think this should be done on the proxy layer where possible since that is where doing `eval(repr(j))` will actually work due to the proxy constructors taking schema items. However, the heavy lifting should be done behind the proxy to stop the proxy layer getting too thick.

Plumbum is designed so it can allow some smart commands piping and I/O redirection using Python's `|`, `>`, `>>` operators but this require that a command instance be created, for example:

```
grep = client.machine['grep']
echo = client.machine['echo']

(echo["Something"] | grep['Something'])()
```

That is great when you are able to define the commands somewhere and then reuse.

On Pulp Smash tests something a basic piping or I/O redirection is needed and instead of creating the command library with many different arguments is easier to access Plumbum machine session and do the piping or I/O redirection passing as a string like:

```
client.machine.session().run('echo Something | grep Something') which is clean and easy to do.

> Side note: Unfortunately Mesh Header is constructed in a way that Deep Hops Left is injected in the middle of the header, so when we forward a frame and the Hops Left is 15, then we can do memmove of payload to save one byte or just use Deep Hops Left until end (thoughts?)

In addition to not requiring `memmove`, keeping the Deep Hops Left field can help give some indication that the packet may have started with a large Hops Left value.  I'm not sure saving 1 byte on the air will make much practical difference.  But I don't have a strong opinion either way.

Some comments:

- you need to add the PYBIND11_NOEXCEPT_TPL_ARG macro to the end of the template arguments, as done in `cpp_function`.  Doing so adds another template parameter when compiled in C++17 mode, which is what the PYBIND11_NOEXCEPT_SPECIFIER contains.  (In C++11/14 these macros are both empty).

- a logical place for this would be as a static function in the `gil_scoped_release` class rather than as a free function.  `wrap` seems a suitable name, so that you would call: `m.def("f", py::gil_scoped_release::wrap(&myfunc))` to bind it.

- `std::function` has some overhead and storage costs which is why it isn't currently used in pybind11.  It ought to be possible to avoid it by basically doing what the 4 constructors in `cpp_function` do.  (They might also be covering some cases, like the const/non-const method detection that you might want to copy).

- I have a feeling that you would need a special case for `Return = void` because C++ doesn't allow `return void`.

This seems a surprisingly hard problem.  The main issue is that the C++ `Clone` and the Python `Clone` do fundamentally different things: the former returns a new C++ instance, the latter returns a new Python instance.  The way referencing works is that the Python instance extends the C++ instance, rather than the other way around, which means casting to the C++ type isn't sufficient to keep the Python instance alive: you can easily end up being the only holder of the C++ instance, as happens here.  (I don't *think* this will segfault: I think overload lookups will simply always fail in such a case since the owning object no longer exists).

The `PyBase` class *could* hold a `py::object` reference to the constructed object (you'd have to expand the `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD` and change it to save the `auto o`--which here is `py::object`), but then you've got a circular reference that can't be automatically cleaned up.

As I said, I don't see an easy way around this.

I'd like to point out that pip 9 aggressively auto-updates itself to pip 10 at every opportunity unless it's explicitly told to hold back, so uptake of pip 10 should be happening quickly. You have to enter something like ``pip install --upgrade mypackage 'pip < 10'`` every time you do something in pip, to tell pip 9 to hold back. 

pip also ignores a setup.py directive to avoid version 10 (``install_requires=['pip < 10']``) and will update or attempt installation with 10 anyway. So there's no workaround for a package to declare itself dependent on pip 9. I hope this underscores the urgency.

@konradkonrad 

In Raiden we have two channels:
A->B [10,0] and B->A [10,0]. 

So if Alice sends tokens to Bob then:
A->B [9,1] and B->A [10,0]. 
A->B [8,2] and B->A [10,0]. 
A->B [7,3] and B->A [10,0]. 
A->B [6,4] and B->A [10,0]. 

So only one channel is updated. If A->B runs out of ether, then we either go into credit, or we close the channel. 

If we include a nonce, then we can have a bidirectional channel:
A<->B and [A,B,c], where A is Alice's balance, B is Bob's balance, and c is the counter. 
A->B and [10,10,1]. 
A->B and [9,11,2].
A->B and [8,12,3].
A<-B and [9,11,4].
A<-B and [10,10,5].

If Alice closes the channel with [8,12,3], then Bob has one opportunity to broadcast  [10,10,5]. 
It still allows for the courtesy close, while giving the counterparty one opportunity to broadcast the latest state.

@mitar , there're two ways to do this.
1. `exec ray start --redis-port 6379` in your dockerfile, so that Ray starts when the docker container is brought up. Then, change all of your scripts running inside your docker container to `ray.init(redis_address=127.0.0.1:6379)`. This option basically assumes that the ray cluster has already been started. Pro: no Ray code changes required.
2. Minor code change in the implementation of `ray.init` to check if port 6379 is open or closed. If closed, assume it's Ray's Redis shard and continue initialization with `redis_address=127.0.0.1:6379`. If open, assume Ray hasn't been started yet. The disadvantage here is that you have to make several assumptions (e.g., that port 6379 (Redis default port) actually belongs to an instance of Ray already running. As Robert mentioned, this would also require a couple of lines of python to check the port.

I think option #1 is a better one. You control the Redis port and can change it, if you want. Fewer assumptions, no Ray code change, one extra argument passed to `ray.init` in your existing scripts, and all scripts are symmetric (all attach to an existing Ray instance).

Hmm, tricky. The underlying operating systems do expose incremental reads for large directories. And Python even exposes it via `os.scandir`. So ideally in the future `iterdir` might use this. (Also, it'd be nice if pathlib exposed the richer `scandir` interface too: it gives you [`DirEntry`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.DirEntry) objects, which have more information that just the name.) So even if right now `iterdir` just naively calls `listdir` and loads up all the results at once, there's an argument that we should make this an async iterator for future-proofing, so that we have the option to switch to using incremental disk reads in the future.

There are some extra complications though:

* `os.scandir` holds open a file handle that has to be closed eventually. If `iterdir` used it, then users would have to be careful to close `iterdir` to avoid a `ResourceWarning` on CPython / fd leak on PyPI. Not necessarily a big deal, but adding this requirement later would be a pretty subtle change.

* The theoretical benefit of using an incremental OS API for reading the directory is that if you have a tremendously huge directory, we don't have to load the full list into memory all at once. But the naive version, where you keep calling `run_sync_in_worker_thread(scandir.__next__)`, will be *incredibly* slow, because you're adding a thread dispatch overhead for every file, even though the vast majority of them don't have to hit the disk. What you really want to do is amortize this by using a single call to `run_sync_in_worker_thread` to load a whole batch of results into memory all at once. Which... is basically what `iterdir` is already doing, with its naive use of `listdir`. In fact using `listdir` like this is probably optimal for 99.9% of cases, because it's extraordinarily rare that you have a directory listing that's so large that simply holding the list of names causes serious memory problems.

....So I think maybe I've convinced myself that `trio.Path.iterdir` should keep using `listdir`, and then potentially in the future we could imagine adding a `trio.Path.scandir` or something with a cleverer batching algorithm, `DirEntry` support, and an API that nudges you to close the `scandir` fd.

-----

Proposed TODO list for this bug

* [ ] Add a note to the docs for `trio.Path.iterdir`, warning people that despite having `iter` in the name it does load up the whole list into memory (by keeping the call signature the same, we're committing to never changing that, so it makes sense to document)

* [ ] Call `listdir` immediately before returning the iterator, like @kyrias suggested

* [ ] Fix another obvious bug while we're at it: currently `trio.Path.iterdir` yields `pathlib.Path` objects, not `trio.Path` objects :-)

Here's an idea: what if we scope async generators to the task that first iterated them? That is:
- each task contains an `_asyncgens` attribute containing a weakref list (can be initially None to avoid having a bunch of empty sets for the tasks that don't use asyncgens)
- we install a firstiter hook that adds a weakref to the new async generator to the `_asyncgens` list on the current task
- when a child task of `nursery` exits, we start a task in the nursery that does the equivalent of `await ag.aclose()` for each of its still-alive asyncgens, in reverse order of firstiter

There's some subtlety here as far as treating the nested child sorta-task like a task for these purposes, and scoping asyncgens created there to the direct-parent nursery rather than to the lifetime of the overarching task that opened that nursery, but I think that's mostly details.

This is only a partial solution; it still doesn't deal correctly with asyncgens that create nurseries/cancel scopes.
```
async def agen():
    with trio.move_on_after(6):
        for i in range(100):
            yield i
            await trio.sleep(i * 0.1)

async def caller():
    ag = agen
    last = None
    while True:
        with trio.move_on_after(0.8) as scope:
            last = await ag.anext()
        await trio.sleep(1)
        if scope.cancelled_caught:
            break
```
From reading the discussion in #264, it seems like the desired behavior here would be to limit each turn of agen() to 0.8 seconds, and the whole thing (including time spent by caller() when agen() is suspended) to 6 seconds. That gives you a shorter-lived cancel scope (in caller()) more deeply in the cancel scope stack than a longer-lived one (in agen()). I guess context variables are supposed to solve this? I feel vaguely like it should be solvable without them, but can't currently think of how. I guess cancel scopes could detect that they were created inside an asyncgen (via... stack introspection? ugh) and mark themselves accordingly, and cancel scope `__exit__` could know it might need to reorder such scopes and delivery of cancels would skip them if the asyncgen is suspended due to a `yield`. This gets really complicated though. Thoughts?

Hmm, this has come up a few times, and it's a totally reasonable question – I think we need some sort of FAQ entry for this... maybe in the tutorial? Will think about it.

Anyway, here's the problem: a function like `run` has to take the thing to run, and it also has to take its own arguments to configure how it runs it. So we need some way to distinguish which arguments are for which, but all the possible solutions involve some kind of awkwardness.

BTW, in ordinary usage you wouldn't use `trio.run` to call `request`, that wouldn't make much sense – you normally call `trio.run` once at the very top of your program, so it's usually something like `trio.run(main)`. (I guess you're looking at making synchronous shim wrappers or something?) But there are a bunch of functions in trio that have this same problem: `nursery.start_soon`, `run_sync_in_worker_thread`, etc. etc., so we do need a general solution.

What we do is recommend people do `from functools import partial` and then `trio.run(partial(request, method, url, pool=my_pool))` or whatever. I'm not a *huge* fan, but all the alternatives seem worse. Some advantages of this are: (a) it works uniformly for async and sync callables, (b) it's ordinary standard Python, so once you learn it  you can use it everywhere, with or without trio, (c) it's introspectable, so e.g. if you start a task like this and then later use trio's debugging interfaces to visualize the task tree, then you'll see that trio has figured out that this task is running a function named `request`, not one named `partial` or `<lambda>` or something, (d) it allows extra arguments to be added, which is kind of a niche thing but important for [`nursery.start`](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#trio.The%20nursery%20interface.start) (note: different from `nursery.start_soon`), (e) the syntax really isn't that bad, and in particular supports all of Python's normal funcall syntax like `partial(request, *args, extra_arg, something=whatever, **my_kwargs)`. The left edge looks a little funny, but otherwise it's using function call syntax to represent a function call.

Rejected alternatives include:

* YOLO it up: `run` takes its arguments, any unrecognized arguments are passed on to the called function. But if we did this, then we could never add any new kwargs to `run` (or similar functions) without breaking backcompat, which isn't workable in a foundational library like trio that needs to (eventually) be super careful about backcompat.

* Reserve underscored kwarg names as being for `trio.run`, and the rest get passed on to the function being called: this is kinda gross, and doesn't really solve the problem, because there's nothing that says an arbitrary function can't take kwargs that start with underscores. In particular, by implementing this we would be defining a dozen such functions! And some of them you might want to pass to each other in unusual-but-they-happen situations.

* Use a special argument for arguments to `run`, like `def run(fn, *args, **kwargs, run_args={})`: this is super awkward to use and document (what arguments can you pass to `run_args`), plus still has the issue of potential kwarg name collisions.

* Use a special arguments for kwargs for the function, like `def run(fn, *args, *, fn_kwargs={}, ...)`: this is uglier to use and read than `partial` (since now you have to write kwargs using dict syntax, while partial uses call syntax), and it's still an idiosyncratic special trick we'd be forcing people to learn. If we're going to make people learn an idiosyncratic trick anyway, `partial` is much more generally useful.

* Allow raw coroutine objects, like `def run(fn(*args, **kwargs), ...)`: it doesn't solve the problem for variants that take sync functions like `run_sync_in_worker_thread`, it doesn't work for more sophisticated `nursery`-like objects that need to control how they run the function passed to `nursery.start_soon` (think: Erlang-style supervisors that can restart services after a crash), and it would make it impossible for us to ever fix the super-annoying wart that new users always run into where they forget a mandatory `await` and Python doesn't give them a proper error.

If you've got a better idea definitely do let me know but... what we do now really is the best option available, AFAICT :-).

There is nothing about the basic auth (even if it is supported bu Kube as explain on their official doc : https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/authentication/ "Kubernetes uses client certificates, bearer tokens, an authenticating proxy, or HTTP basic auth to authenticate....")

Second I check in the code and the method "get_basic_auth_token" in configuration.py is never call anywhere (and it is the only one dealing with username/password field). 

Then I try to "hack" a little the python code by modifying the class configuration and change its auth_setting with that

```
    def auth_settings(self):
        """
        Gets Auth Settings dict for api client.

        :return: The Auth Settings information dict.
        """
        return {
            'BearerToken':
                {
                    'type': 'api_key',
                    'in': 'header',
                    'key': 'authorization',

                'http_basic_test':
                {
                    'type': 'basic',
                    'in': 'header',

                },
        }

I just added the "http_basic_test" here. Then you can take any functional class like ""core_v1_api and modify the method you plan to use (list_pod_for_all_namespaces_with_http_info in my case) and modify the auth part of the code. Replace 
`auth_settings = ['BearerToken']`

`auth_settings = ['http_basic_test']`

I wonder how people are using `--footprints` - or should be using it.

I imagine you want to have the footprints when you download a limited set of files that you will keep in a designated folder, like `my_s2_data_for_project_A`. Then it makes a lot of sense to store the footprints file together with those files so you quickly can see the data coverage and find the right data file. And I guess then it does not matter so much what the file is called, since you have it in a unique directory and it will likely be the only `.geojson` file in that directory.

But it could also be that you want the footprints prior to downloading to evaluate the data coverage and decide which files to actually download. In that case, you might want to store them all in one directory under unique names like `footprints_L1C.geojson` and `footprints_L2A.geojson`. Then you indeed need to be able to specify the filename / full path.

So I guess the easiest is indeed to have the user specify a path and add the extension automatically, just like the initial suggestion.

@lsorber : The problem is that when the python process exits it cleans up all the python global objects including the nmslib index you created. The NMSLIB python IndexWrapper object was logging that it was being deleted, which called back into the python logging system. The python logger threw a python exception (since it had already been partially cleaned up), which pybind11 re-threw as a c++ exception. Since the log call was in a C++ destructor this caused the SIGABORT you saw.

@searchivarius : In this particular case,  I think we should just ensure that the logger doesn't throw an exception (attempted in #332). We could also remove the log calls from all destructors, I think I just added that one to help out with that python memory leak iirc.
